title: Solateria
anchor_summary: A hand-drawn action metroidvania built around precise parry-focused combat, boss fights, exploration-driven ability gating, and character build customization in a fallen fantasy world.

similar_games.title: Nine Sols
similar_games.why_similar: 2D metroidvania with a major emphasis on timing-based deflection/parry combat, boss duels, exploration, and ability-driven progression.

similar_games.title: Hollow Knight
similar_games.why_similar: Benchmark combat-heavy metroidvania with interconnected exploration, tough bosses, map discovery, and gradual power unlocks.

similar_games.title: Blasphemous 2
similar_games.why_similar: Side-scrolling action metroidvania focused on demanding melee combat, traversal unlocks, boss encounters, and dark fallen-world storytelling.

similar_games.title: Prince of Persia: The Lost Crown
similar_games.why_similar: Fast, polished metroidvania centered on precision combat, parries, mobility upgrades, and a guided-but-open world structure.

similar_games.title: Ender Lilies: Quietus of the Knights
similar_games.why_similar: Atmospheric combat-focused metroidvania with boss-gated progression, build customization, and a ruined world explored through nonlinear upgrades.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic text strongly and repeatedly identify Solateria as an 'action Metroidvania' with hand-drawn side-scrolling exploration, hidden areas unlocked through skills, shortcuts, free-order world exploration, and boss-focused parry combat. The core loop centers on traversing an interconnected world, acquiring new abilities/upgrades, uncovering secrets, and defeating powerful bosses, which cleanly fits metroidvania over RPG alternatives. The parry timing, difficulty tuning, and pattern-learning boss fights also support a soulslike-flavored secondary reading, but the dominant identity is exploration-platforming progression in a side-scrolling campaign.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: 2D metroidvania with a major emphasis on timing-based deflection/parry combat, boss duels, exploration, and ability-driven progression.

title: Collector's Cove

similar_games.title: Summer in Mara
similar_games.why_similar: Cozy farming and crafting loop centered on sailing between islands, gathering resources, growing crops, and light quest-driven exploration.

similar_games.title: Havendock
similar_games.why_similar: Sea-based life sim focused on building and personalizing a floating home, farming, crafting, and relaxed resource management.

similar_games.title: Moonglow Bay
similar_games.why_similar: Gentle fishing-heavy progression with collection goals, crafting, and tranquil exploration rather than combat pressure.

similar_games.title: Stardew Valley
similar_games.why_similar: Recognizable cozy farming template with crop cultivation, fishing, collecting, crafting, and steady completion-driven progression.

similar_games.title: Animal Crossing: New Horizons
similar_games.why_similar: Relaxed collecting, fishing, crafting, customization, and low-stakes daily progression that matches the cozy, non-combat appeal.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Collector's Cove as a cozy, non-combat farming adventure centered on cultivating a floating farm, fishing, crafting, personalizing a ship-home, exploring islands, collecting entries for a compendium, and bonding with an animal companion. Multiple sources repeat the same core loop and explicitly state 'There are no enemies in this world,' which strongly supports a farming/life-sim identity over action or survival archetypes. The game appears single-player, casual, long-form, and exploration-driven, with progression through farm growth, collection, personalization, and companion relationship building.

similar_games.why_similar: Another near-identical modern WWE 2K entry centered on realistic pro wrestling matches, licensed roster play, Showcase content, and deep sandbox/creation modes.

similar_games.why_similar: Modern foundation for the current WWE 2K formula, mixing arcade-accessible controls with simulation presentation, licensed wrestlers, and extensive custom creation.

similar_games.title: AEW: Fight Forever
similar_games.why_similar: Closest major non-WWE wrestling comp: a roster-based pro wrestling game focused on match cards, multiplayer bouts, wrestler movesets, and ring-based combat progression.

Evidence repeatedly mentions new match types, PvP and co-op support, Showcase/MyRISE single-player campaigns, Universe/MyGM management layers, and MyFACTION team competition. Steam labels include Simulation and Sports, while the copy emphasizes winning matches, realistic wrestling presentation, and replayable seasonal/live-service-adjacent content. Although wrestling is not a dedicated archetype in the allowed list, sports_sim is the best fit for its core gameplay identity.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a third-person cinematic action-adventure centered on a linear heroic rescue quest through semi-open historical-medieval Italy. Core play is melee sword combat with two combat stances, boss-oriented encounters, authored story progression, exploration of handcrafted locations, and light collection/progression via skill books and weapon customization. This strongly fits cinematic_action_adventure; character-action and soulslike traits are present but less central because the material emphasizes cinematic narrative journey and setpiece adventure over pure combo mastery or explicitly punishing soulslike structure.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define Solasta II as a 'Turn-Based Tactical RPG' built around a faithful tabletop-style ruleset, with the player creating and guiding a party of 4 adventurers through a quest-driven campaign. The evidence strongly supports party control, tactical party-based combat, quest/exploration progression, and choice-driven CRPG structure. The text also indicates freer roaming across Neokos rather than purely linear mission flow, which fits a semi-open campaign CRPG better than a pure tactics game. Turn-based tactics is a strong secondary identity, but the party creation, roleplay framing, questing, and choice emphasis make party_based_crpg the better primary archetype.

title: John Carpenter's Toxic Commando
anchor_summary: A squad-based co-op horde shooter built around four-player teamwork, class/character choice, constant infected swarms, and vehicle-assisted runs through semi-open maps.

similar_games.title: World War Z: Aftermath
similar_games.why_similar: The clearest comp: 4-player co-op missions, enormous zombie swarms via Saber’s swarm tech, class-based loadouts, and objective-driven horde shooting.

similar_games.title: Back 4 Blood
similar_games.why_similar: Modern 4-player zombie co-op shooter with team synergy, specials mixed into large hordes, and run-based progression/loadout building.

similar_games.title: Left 4 Dead 2
similar_games.why_similar: A foundational squad zombie shooter focused on moving through campaigns together, revives, resource sharing, and surviving heavy infected pressure.

similar_games.title: Killing Floor 2
similar_games.why_similar: Wave-based co-op FPS centered on class perks, heavy gunplay, and mowing down dense monster hordes with a coordinated team.

similar_games.title: Zombie Army 4: Dead War
similar_games.why_similar: Co-op undead shooting across campaign stages with set-piece swarm fights, squad support, and progression that emphasizes fighting through hordes together.

The core loop reads much closer to cooperative horror-action than to military FPS, hero shooter, or arena FPS. Tone is pulpy rather than pure horror, but the infected/undead threat, survival framing, and squad-based horde combat strongly support co_op_horror as the closest taxonomy fit.

title: Piece By Piece
anchor_summary: A cozy shop-management sim built around hands-on repair puzzles: restoring customers’ treasured objects, customizing and decorating the shop, tending small side tasks like gardening/paint mixing, and selling refreshed goods in a wholesome village setting.

similar_games.title: Assemble with Care
similar_games.why_similar: Centered on carefully taking apart and repairing sentimental household objects through tactile puzzle interactions.

similar_games.title: FixFox
similar_games.why_similar: A wholesome repair-and-fetch loop focused on fixing quirky items for clients, with light crafting and cozy worldbuilding.

similar_games.title: House Flipper 2
similar_games.why_similar: Shares the satisfying clean-up, restoration, decoration, and resale loop, even though it focuses on homes instead of small keepsakes.

similar_games.title: Potion Craft: Alchemist Simulator
similar_games.why_similar: A cozy workshop sim about serving customers, customizing outputs, and running a small handcrafted business for profit.

similar_games.title: Garden Paws
similar_games.why_similar: Blends cute animal-town vibes with shopkeeping, crafting, decorating, and low-stress daily chores in a cozy life-sim structure.

payload.anchor_summary: A cozy shop-management sim built around hands-on repair puzzles: restoring customers’ treasured objects, customizing and decorating the shop, tending small side tasks like gardening/paint mixing, and selling refreshed goods in a wholesome village setting.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store text consistently describes a cozy single-player shop-life loop: inherit and run a family repair shop, restore customer items, grow berries for paint, decorate the shop, bake cookies, clean and maintain the workspace, and interact with neighbors. The core identity is non-combat, low-pressure domestic/occupational simulation with customization and social-town flavor, which fits life_sim best. Repairing objects 'each with its own puzzle' adds a light hidden-object/puzzle-adjacent layer, while selling items and maintaining the store gives a management secondary aspect, but the evidence most strongly supports a cozy life-sim structure rather than a pure tycoon or puzzle game.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Centered on carefully taking apart and repairing sentimental household objects through tactile puzzle interactions.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored description clearly identifies the game as a 'brainteasing precision platformer' built around single-screen challenges across 50 unique levels. Core play is running and leaping through increasingly complex stages, with explicit platforming plus puzzle gating via freezing time, shifting perspectives, pushing crates, activating switches, and using the cat companion's body as an improvised platform. This strongly supports a precision_platformer classification with level-based, side-scrolling 2D, non-combat gameplay.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Puzzle-platforming centered on precise movement and time manipulation to solve compact stage challenges.
Single-screen-style puzzle stages built around coordinating different characters’ abilities to progress.

Single-player campaign structure is directly supported, and the village mystery plus ghost encounters suggest a guided exploratory survival-horror loop.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies Galactic Vault as a single-player first-person shooter roguelite with fast-paced combat, permanent arsenal expansion, buildcraft via weapon attachments and upgrade combinations, boss encounters, and room-based infiltration through vaults. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype set does not contain a clear FPS roguelite / boomer-shooter-roguelite bucket. The strongest candidate, arena_fps, is not well supported because the game is not described as match-based or arena-only and instead emphasizes single-player roguelite progression. Immersive_sim_action is also not sufficiently supported because the text does not establish systemic problem solving, multiple infiltration approaches, or immersive-sim interaction depth. Rejecting due to taxonomy fit gap rather than lack of gameplay evidence.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Fast first-person roguelite runs with high mobility, room-to-room combat, build synergies, bosses, and meta progression.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently and repeatedly identify Haunted Lands as a retro-inspired action platformer. The evidence strongly supports a side-scrolling, level-based single-player campaign built around platform traversal and dominant combat: players slay hordes of cultists and monsters, use six distinct characters with different close-range and ranged playstyles, acquire artifacts that alter abilities, uncover secrets, and fight challenging bosses. Horror setting and bleak/grotesque tone are explicit, but the core gameplay identity is much more action-platformer than horror-specialist archetypes. The prior computed candidate pointing to sports_sim is clearly spurious and contradicted by all textual evidence.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly establishes a cooperative multiplayer add-on with two-player story missions, four-player survival matches, class selection, map-based territory control, and wave-based enemy defense in a supernatural setting. However, the available text does not firmly establish a supported taxonomy archetype from the allowed list. It does not provide reliable evidence for perspective, traversal, long-term progression, or roguelite structure, and the DLC context does not include a parent game to inherit core gameplay identity from. The mode appears closer to a co-op wave/survival action experience, but that exact archetype is not available in the taxonomy, so assigning a nearest-fit archetype would be too speculative.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently identify the core loop as a 'chaotic Roguelite Twin-Stick Shooter with a Base Defense Twist' playable with up to 4 players. The text describes repeated runs with time reversal, retaining experience/resources between attempts, which strongly supports roguelite run structure plus metaprogression. Moment-to-moment play centers on exploring a planet, gathering resources, fighting monsters in arena encounters and waves, then returning to defend the crashed ship using crafted ramparts, turrets, and traps. Combat is dominant, fast, and top-down/twin-stick in identity, with multiple class/loadout styles spanning melee, explosives, flamethrowers, turrets, missiles, dashes, and ranged attacks. Co-op is explicitly emphasized in Steam categories and main copy, making co_op_action_roguelite the best fit. The base-defense/crafting layer is meaningful but additive rather than enough to displace the action-roguelite identity.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Squad-based roguelite shooter where runs revolve around escorting and defending a core objective while balancing combat, upgrades, and wave pressure.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Mr. Sleepy Man as a 3D platformer with an expressive moveset ('jump, glide, and dive'), interconnected levels, heavy exploration, secrets, collectibles, and hundreds of objectives. The core loop centers on platform navigation, roaming semi-open handcrafted spaces, interacting with objects/NPCs, and completing collectathon-style objectives rather than combat. The tone is overtly comedic and whimsical, matching a 3D collectathon identity more strongly than a metroidvania. Action-platformer is a secondary fit because of the movement-focused traversal and chaos-oriented play, but collectible/exploration structure is the clearer primary gameplay identity.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Crabmeat as a short first-person, mouse-only, point-and-click survival horror experience about operating a crabbing vessel, collecting quota within 7 days, navigating Antarctic waters, maintaining traps/ship functions, and defending against underwater threats. That is enough to infer a horror adventure with light survival/vehicle-management elements, but it does not cleanly map to any single allowed archetype. The current computed hidden_object_puzzle candidate appears overfit to point-and-click/puzzle signals and is not well supported by the actual described loop, while survival_horror/psychological_horror are also not securely established because the evidence does not clearly describe the genre-defining combat/resource/scare structure expected for those archetypes. Strong evidence exists for a hybrid first-person point-and-click horror adventure, but not for a confident placement into the available taxonomy archetypes.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Granblue Fantasy as a classic JRPG with a deep turn-based combat system, elemental strategy, classes, party-building, character/weapon progression, and a main quest plus character story episodes. The text strongly supports a story-driven, party-command RPG identity over any other candidate. Real-time co-op raids are present, but they read as an additive mode layered onto the core authored campaign JRPG structure rather than replacing it. The fighting/match-based signals in the prior computation appear to be false positives from words like raids, combos, and co-op rather than evidence of a traditional fighter.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a single-player, turn-based, party-commanded dungeon-crawling roguelike RPG with permadeath, procedural generation, recruitable characters, and skill learning. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not cleanly fit this identity. The evidence does not establish key archetype-defining traits needed for turn_based_tactics or tactical_rpg, such as tactical-overhead presentation, explicit battlefield structure, or clearer tactical-RPG conventions; nor does it support jrpg_story_rpg, party_based_crpg, or card_battler. Because the gameplay is real but sits between supported archetypes and the strongest candidate mapping would be too inferential, classification should be rejected rather than forced.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: Party-based turn-based dungeon expeditions with heavy attrition, permadeath pressure, roster management, and grim exploratory progression.
Anime-styled turn-based roguelike dungeon crawler focused on leading a full party through procedurally dangerous labyrinth runs.
A classic labyrinth-focused party dungeon RPG with turn-based combat, exploration, and party-building, though less run-based and less roguelike.
Traditional roguelike structure with turn-based combat, procedural dungeons, permadeath options, and deep skill progression in a retro presentation.

title: Age of Wonders 4: Rise from Ruin
anchor_summary: Add-on content for Age of Wonders 4, extending its fantasy 4X empire-building and turn-based tactical battles with new cultures, tomes, map traits, and realm challenges.

similar_games.title: Age of Wonders 4
similar_games.why_similar: The parent game; this DLC directly inherits its fantasy 4X strategy loop, empire customization, and turn-based tactical combat.

similar_games.title: Age of Wonders: Planetfall
similar_games.why_similar: Another Triumph 4X that pairs strategic empire management with separate turn-based tactical battles and faction customization.

similar_games.title: Age of Wonders III
similar_games.why_similar: Very close series match with fantasy realm expansion, hero-led armies, city building, and tactical turn-based encounters.

similar_games.title: Endless Legend
similar_games.why_similar: Fantasy 4X focused on expanding cities, researching faction-specific powers, and fighting tactical turn-based battles on the map.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted using DLC inheritance from the parent game because the DLC description is additive only: it adds a new culture, form, tomes, wildlife, terrain, quests, and realm traits, and does not describe a different core mode or genre. Parent-game evidence explicitly describes 'Age of Wonders’ signature blend of 4X strategy and turn-based tactical combat,' empire expansion over turns, realm exploration, customizable factions, city/empire growth, tactical battles, and replayable realm generation. The DLC copy reinforces the same identity with battlefield mobility, territory seizure, harvesting ancient wonders, new world-map content, epic quests, and new tomes. This strongly supports 4x_strategy as primary, with turn-based tactical battles as a secondary gameplay layer.

resolved_similar_games.why_similar: The parent game; this DLC directly inherits its fantasy 4X strategy loop, empire customization, and turn-based tactical combat.

title: Fly for Fly
anchor_summary: A light, comedic action game about controlling a tiny flying pest, weaving around a confined environment, provoking humans, and surviving their swats through timing and movement.

similar_games.title: Mr. Moskeeto
similar_games.why_similar: Arcade-style insect play focused on buzzing around humans, avoiding swats, and using nimble movement in small spaces.

similar_games.title: Untitled Goose Game
similar_games.why_similar: Centers on irritating people and escalating their frustration through mischievous actions in a comedic stealth-chaos loop.

similar_games.title: Rain on Your Parade
similar_games.why_similar: Short-form prank gameplay built around causing escalating environmental annoyance and chaos with simple controls.

similar_games.title: Bee Simulator
similar_games.why_similar: Shares third-person insect flight, obstacle avoidance, and navigating human-scale environments from a tiny creature perspective.

similar_games.title: Goat Simulator
similar_games.why_similar: A slapstick chaos game where the core appeal is disrupting ordinary spaces and provoking funny reactions through movement-driven antics.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is limited to a single short Metacritic-style description describing a simple comedic action game where the player controls a fly, dodges swats, and annoys students in a classroom. While this suggests arcade-style evasive action with timing-heavy movement, the evidence does not strongly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. It does not clearly establish platforming, shooter structure, racing, horror, RPG systems, puzzle structure, tactics, sim management, or another supported core gameplay identity. Because the gameplay appears to fall outside the available archetype set and the source base is sparse, rejection is more reliable than forcing an imprecise fit.

title: Packing Life
anchor_summary: A cozy single-player spatial-organization puzzle game about rotating, arranging, and efficiently fitting varied items into limited box space, with light sim framing, progression rewards, and a relaxing presentation.

similar_games.title: Unpacking
similar_games.why_similar: Cozy item-placement puzzling built around organizing personal objects into constrained spaces with a calm, satisfying loop.

similar_games.title: A Little to the Left
similar_games.why_similar: Casual organization puzzle game focused on sorting, aligning, and neatly arranging everyday items for visual and spatial satisfaction.

similar_games.title: Wilmot's Warehouse
similar_games.why_similar: Warehouse-themed organization game centered on categorizing, storing, and efficiently retrieving items within limited space.

similar_games.title: Assemble with Care
similar_games.why_similar: Relaxed object-focused puzzle game with tactile manipulation, light narrative framing, and a soothing, accessible structure.

similar_games.title: Save Room - Organization Puzzle
similar_games.why_similar: Inventory-management puzzler about rotating and fitting differently shaped objects into tight spaces for optimal layouts.

llm_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence strongly supports a cozy single-player non-combat puzzle/simulation game centered on organizing, rotating, and fitting items into boxes under space constraints, with light story and workspace customization. However, that gameplay identity does not cleanly map to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype. The computed near-match of hidden_object_puzzle is not well supported by the text, which describes spatial packing/optimization rather than hidden object search, point-and-click investigation, or mystery solving. Because the available evidence is strong but indicates an out-of-taxonomy organization/packing puzzle rather than an existing archetype, rejection is more appropriate than forcing an inaccurate fit.

Inventory-management puzzler about rotating and fitting differently shaped objects into tight spaces for optimal layouts.
